<!-- Generated by the rag-architecture-review research workflow (9 agents: 5 research strands
→ synthesis → 2 adversarial critics → finalize), 2026-06-14. Cloudflare Workers AI catalog
claims were re-checked by a dedicated fact-checking critic against the live docs — but model
ids/pricing move fast, so re-verify before acting on a model swap. -->

# Rules Goblin RAG — Architecture & Model Review (June 2026)

## 1. Executive summary

- **Your pipeline is already best-in-class for its shape.** A closed-corpus, single-game-scoped, single-hop factual RAG over short rulebooks, running embed → ANN → cosine noise-floor → D1 hydrate → cross-encoder rerank → gate → generate, with a deterministic no-generation-call refusal path, is exactly the design every 2026 benchmark and production study recommends for this use case. The recently-fixed reranker-as-the-real-gate decision (cosine probed live at 0.555 for the correct Monopoly Jail passage vs 0.583 for an unrelated rent rule) is the textbook fix for the known dense-retrieval failure mode. You are not behind.
- **Stay deterministic. Do not go agentic.** Agentic RAG costs materially more input and output tokens per query, adds several seconds of p95 latency, and — decisively — any agent that uses the LLM to *decide scope* destroys your single biggest cost lever: today an out-of-scope query never reaches the generation model. Your domain (one game per session, single-hop questions, closed corpus) has none of the failure modes agentic RAG fixes.
- **Be precise about what the refusal path costs.** This is the load-bearing claim under the whole anti-abuse argument, so state it exactly: an out-of-scope, rate-limited, or over-budget reply skips the **expensive generation call** (`@cf/meta/llama-3.3-70b-instruct-fp8-fast`, $2.25/M out) **and the daily-budget slot** — but it is NOT free of all model calls. The session burst limiter (`MSG_LIMITER`, agent.ts:110) and the per-IP limiter (index.ts) gate *before* any retrieval, so a throttled message costs zero. A message that *clears* the limiters but is off-topic still runs at least one billed `bge-m3` embed + a Vectorize query, the two-pass retry adds a second embed + query, and any pass that clears the 0.15 cosine floor also runs the billed `bge-reranker-base` call — all before the 0-survivor refusal at agent.ts:133. The real abuse ceiling is the rate limiters, not zero cost. Also note the 5000/day budget breaker counts **only in-scope queries** (agent.ts:137-150 runs after the out-of-scope return), so an off-topic flood is capped by the per-colo rate limits alone, not the daily budget. This makes the anti-agentic case *stronger*, not weaker — agentic would stack generation calls on top of this.
- **The one genuine, available quality gap is hybrid retrieval.** You run bge-m3 dense-only and discard the sparse signal the same forward pass already produces (Workers AI exposes dense only — there is an open feature request for `return_sparse`/`return_dense`/`return_colbert_vecs`, so it is not available today). The practical, zero-extra-inference fix is a D1 FTS5 BM25 leg fused with the ANN results by RRF (use k=10–20, not the TREC-default 60, on a 30–80 chunk corpus). This targets exactly the exact-game-term mis-rankings cosine gets wrong, runs entirely on Cloudflare, and leaves the immutable Vectorize index untouched.
- **Make `--contextual` the default and re-index existing rulebooks — but gate it on the eval first.** Contextual blurbs cut top-20 retrieval failure substantially in Anthropic's study (their headline is up to ~49% fewer failed retrievals, ~67% with reranking), and rulebook prose is unusually dependent on cross-references ("draw from the bag", "after setup"). It is an ingest-time cost only, paid once per rulebook. But it is not "just remove the flag": the blurb generator is a sequential per-chunk Moonshot loop, re-indexing re-embeds and re-upserts every vector (irreversible-ish), and the blurb prefix pushes more chunks toward the 1024-token cap (the local tokenizer already undercounts Workers AI's server count ~2.5x, ingest.ts:49-53). Validate before/after Hit-Rate@5 on at least one game before the global default.
- **Keep all four models; two are now sub-optimal-on-paper.** Generation (`llama-3.3-70b-instruct-fp8-fast`) is the right pick for instruction-heavy citation work (IFEval 92.1) and is **explicitly retained** in Cloudflare's May 2026 deprecation changelog (the `-fast` and `-lora` variants stay; the 3.1-70b and 3.1/3-8b line ARE deprecated). Embedding (`bge-m3`) is matched on paper by `@cf/qwen/qwen3-embedding-0.6b` at the same $0.012/M and same 1024-dim/cosine — but the gain is small on English short rulebooks and migrating means a full re-embed and re-upsert of every vector, so hold. Reranker (`bge-reranker-base`) is the only reranker on Workers AI, so the choice is forced.
- **Biggest cost lever is generation, not architecture.** `@cf/google/gemma-4-26b-a4b-it` is $0.30/M output vs $2.25/M for the current model (7.5x cheaper), with 256K context and function calling. It is an eval-gated swap, not a re-architecture — validate against your citation/expansion-scoping prompt before you trust it. For a public no-login app billed per call, this deserves to sit right behind the eval harness that gates it.
- **The real missing thing is measurement, not capability.** You have no gold Q&A set, so every threshold (cosine 0.15, rerank 0.2, top-5) and every fix (expansion scoping, terse-follow-up retry) is currently unguarded by regression tests. Build a per-game gold set and a zero-LLM retrieval eval first; it unlocks every other decision below.

## 2. Scorecard

| Dimension | Rating | Justification |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Chunking | **Best-in-class** | 512-token target / 1024 hard cap / 50 overlap with atomic tables to 1500 is BAAI's own recommendation and matches bge-m3's effective input; tables-kept-whole is a thoughtful rulebook-specific touch. |
| Embedding | **Solid** | bge-m3 (1024/cosine, $0.012/M) is strong; `qwen3-embedding-0.6b` is a same-dims/same-metric/same-price alternative (1024/cosine/$0.012, 4096-token input) that is better on paper, but only the immutable re-embed/re-upsert cost separates them, and the gain is unlikely to be perceptible on a small English corpus. |
| Retrieval / hybrid | **Gap** | Dense-only. Workers AI exposes only bge-m3's dense vector (no `return_sparse`), and you have no BM25 leg, so exact game-term queries (card names, rule numbers) ride on cosine, which you proved mis-ranks this corpus. D1 FTS5 hybrid is the highest-confidence available fix and is first-party + inference-free. |
| Reranking | **Best-in-class** (for the platform) | topK=20 → cross-encoder → keep ≥0.2 → top-5 is correctly tuned for a 30–80 chunk corpus and is the real relevance gate; bge-reranker-base is the only Workers AI reranker (512-token input), so you are at the platform ceiling by necessity. |
| Generation | **Best-in-class** | llama-3.3-70b-fp8-fast has the highest documented instruction-following (IFEval 92.1) in the catalog, is explicitly retained (not deprecated), and the citation + expansion-scoping prompt is in production. Only liability is the **24,000-token Workers AI context window** (verified on the model page; the upstream 128K is not selectable on Workers AI). |
| Contextual retrieval | **Solid** | Implemented (Anthropic-style, kimi-k2.7-code via Moonshot's external API) — ahead of comparable open projects — but it is opt-in (`--contextual`) and not guaranteed applied to all games, so the benefit is unrealised across the corpus. |
| Guardrails | **Best-in-class** | No-generation-call canned replies on out-of-scope / rate-limited / over-budget, per-session + per-IP limiters that gate before retrieval, daily budget cap on in-scope queries, per-session DO isolation, 4h PII wipe. This is the strongest part of the system and the constraint every redesign must preserve. (It bounds abuse cost to embed+Vectorize+rerank within the rate-limit ceiling, not to zero — see §1.) |
| Evaluation | **Gap** | No gold Q&A set, no retrieval metrics, no faithfulness/citation-precision check, no regression guard on the expansion-scoping or terse-follow-up fixes. Every threshold is hand-calibrated on a single live probe. This is the weakest dimension. |

## 3. Model choices

All ids below are verified Workers AI catalog entries against the live Cloudflare docs (June 2026). One correction to the draft: your contextual blurbs run on **`kimi-k2.7-code` via Moonshot's external API** (`api.moonshot.ai`, scripts/ingest.ts:53-55) — but an **in-catalog `@cf/moonshotai/kimi-k2.7-code` does exist** on Workers AI (same $0.95/M in, $4.00/M out, 262K context). The project simply chose to call Moonshot directly. Because this is an offline, ingest-only step, leaving Cloudflare here is acceptable — and a fully first-party path is available if you ever want it.

**Embedding — `@cf/baai/bge-m3` → KEEP, plan a migration trigger.** Well-matched: $0.012/M, 1024/cosine fits the index, 512-token chunks sit inside its window. The honest challenge: `@cf/qwen/qwen3-embedding-0.6b` (same $0.012/M, same 1024/cosine, longer 4096-token input window, stronger on paper) is **drop-in on the immutable Vectorize axes (1024/cosine), so no index re-creation — but still a full re-embed and re-upsert of every existing vector**, which is the real cost. That is an irreversible-ish operational commitment for a gain unlikely to be perceptible on a small English corpus, and the reranker already compensates for cosine mis-rankings. Decision: hold now; migrate immediately if you add non-English rulebooks or hit a measured retrieval-quality wall. Do **not** consider `bge-large-en-v1.5` (17x the price, English-only, 512-token input ceiling, weaker than Qwen3).

**Reranker — `@cf/baai/bge-reranker-base` → KEEP (forced).** It is the only reranker on Workers AI (verified: the AI Search reranking table lists exactly one entry, 512-token input). Its 512-token limit aligns with your 512-token chunks, and the 0.2 sigmoid gate is correctly calibrated (clean gap probed on Monopoly: 0.841 for the right passage, ≤0.0004 for everything else). An external reranker (Cohere Rerank 3, ~$2/M) would break the all-first-party constraint and add data egress for a marginal cross-encoder-vs-cross-encoder delta — not worth it. *If* `@cf/baai/bge-reranker-v2-m3` or `@cf/qwen/qwen3-reranker-0.6b` ever ship (neither is in the catalog today), both would be materially stronger and a zero-friction swap (runtime config only, no re-index) — recalibrate the 0.2 threshold after.

**Generation — `@cf/meta/llama-3.3-70b-instruct-fp8-fast` → KEEP for now; eval Gemma 4 as the cost lever.** Highest IFEval (92.1) in catalog — the dominant quality for "cite as [N], never invent rules, default to base game" — and Cloudflare's May 8 2026 deprecation changelog explicitly lists the `-fast` variant under variants that remain active, so it is the safe long-term Llama pick. One verified live risk: the **24,000-token Workers AI context window** (not the upstream 128K, which is not selectable here). With full history sent and 5 passages + 600 output reserved, a long session can approach the ceiling; this is bounded already by `maxPersistedMessages = 100` (agent.ts:40) and the 4h DO wipe, so it is a reliability edge case, not a cost or guardrail risk. The strongest in-catalog alternative is `@cf/google/gemma-4-26b-a4b-it`: $0.30/M output (7.5x cheaper), 256K context (removes the window concern), function calling. It is newer and unvalidated against your specific citation/scoping prompt, so gate the swap on an eval. On models to skip: `@cf/moonshotai/kimi-k2.6` is **~1.8x the current output cost ($4.00/M vs $2.25/M)** for capabilities the Goblin doesn't use (the draft's "3-4x"/"4x" was overstated). For `@cf/meta/llama-4-scout-17b-16e-instruct`, there is **no documented Workers AI tool-call defect** — Cloudflare's own chat-agent example uses Scout *with* tools — so steer away from it only on the verifiable ground that it is a 17B MoE vs the 70B dense model, i.e. weaker instruction-following for strict [N] citation formatting, not an invented bug.

**Contextual model — `kimi-k2.7-code` (Moonshot, external, ingest-only) → KEEP, make default (gated).** The model choice is fine for a once-per-rulebook offline step. The issue is the policy, not the model: blurbs are opt-in and likely not applied to all games, leaving a proven large retrieval-failure reduction unrealised. Make `--contextual` the default and re-run ingest on existing rulebooks — after validating the gain on one game (see §5, item 4) and pre-checking the blurb-prefix token-budget interaction. If you ever want this fully Cloudflare-native, either the in-catalog `@cf/moonshotai/kimi-k2.7-code` or `@cf/google/gemma-4-26b-a4b-it` could generate the blurbs in-catalog at ingest; there is no urgency at this corpus size.

## 4. Pipeline vs agentic

**Recommendation: stay with the deterministic pipeline. Do not adopt agentic RAG.** This is the correct call on every axis for *this* app.

- **Per-call cost.** On your prices ($0.29/M in, $2.25/M out for llama-3.3-70b), agentic RAG's reported input- and output-token overhead per query directly multiplies your operator subsidy on a public, no-login service. Worse, agentic loops have a fat-tail cost distribution — a misconfigured loop can run several times typical cost — which makes your 5000-query/day budget cap unforecastable. Your current pipeline has a bounded, predictable cost per answered query.
- **Agentic scope decisions stack generation calls onto the cheapest path.** Today, an out-of-scope query returns zero reranker survivors ≥0.2 and is refused with **no generation call** and no daily-budget slot consumed (it still pays embed + Vectorize, possibly rerank — see §1). Any ReAct/Self-RAG/CRAG design where the LLM decides scope requires at least one billed *generation* forward pass *before* it can refuse. At even a 20% off-topic rate, that converts your cheapest path (no $2.25/M-out call) into one where every off-topic message pays one. Preserving the no-generation-call refusal is the single most important cost-control mechanism, and agentic breaks it by construction.
- **None of the failure modes agentic fixes exist here.** Multi-hop synthesis, cross-source routing, ambiguous-intent disambiguation — your corpus is one short rulebook per game_id, queries are single-hop factual lookups, and the only cross-document case (base vs expansion) is *already* handled deterministically: both documents share a game_id, topK=20 + rerank pulls both into the same context, and the prompt layers base/expansion. A well-optimised "Enhanced" pipeline (which yours is — retry, rerank, noise floor, scoping) is reported to match or beat agentic while staying cheaper, with agentic intent-routing backfiring in narrow domains (Ferrazzi et al., "Is Agentic RAG worth it? An experimental comparison of RAG approaches", arXiv:2601.07711 — directional finding; the exact token multipliers and venue are not independently confirmed, so they are not load-bearing here). CRAG's web-search fallback has no target in a closed corpus; Self-RAG needs fine-tuning you can't do on hosted Workers AI models.
- **Latency.** The Goblin is interactive chat; users expect 1–2s. Adding LLM calls in a loop roughly doubles median and pushes p95 to several seconds — an unacceptable UX regression for a lookup where the answer already exists in the rulebook.

**When agentic would actually win:** if you add a feature whose query genuinely spans game_ids with no pre-scoping — e.g. a "compare Quacks vs Potion Explosion base rules" cross-game mode, or open-ended multi-game research. That is a new product surface (sub-query decompose → retrieve game A → retrieve game B → synthesize) where multi-hop retrieval earns its overhead. Even then, gate the retrieval/scope step in deterministic Worker code *before* the agent's first LLM turn so the no-generation-call refusal survives, and use Gemma 4 for routing.

## 5. Prioritized backlog

Eval harness first (it gates everything), then the genuine zero-runtime-cost levers, then the eval-gated quality/cost work, then edge-case reliability.

1. **Build per-game gold Q&A sets (8–15 pairs/game) + a zero-LLM retrieval eval. [P0]** Prerequisite for everything else. Each pair: `{question, conversation_history?, expected_chunk_ids[], reference_answer, category}` across five categories (rule lookup, edge-case, explicit-expansion, base-with-decoy, unanswerable). The eval script reuses the ingest tooling's existing Workers AI + Vectorize REST clients to compute Hit-Rate@5, Recall@20, Precision@5 against chunk IDs — no generation call. Impact: makes every threshold and fix measurable. Effort: M (2–4h/game, once). Measure: the metrics themselves become the baseline.
2. **Add D1 FTS5 BM25 leg + RRF fusion (the real runtime retrieval upgrade). [P1]** CREATE VIRTUAL TABLE chunks_fts; INSERT alongside chunk rows at ingest; at query time run BM25 `WHERE game_id = ?` in parallel with the ANN call and RRF-fuse (k=10–20) before the reranker. No new inference, no re-index, guardrail untouched (rerank gate still decides scope), runs entirely on Cloudflare. Impact: High where cosine mis-ranks exact game terms — your documented failure mode. Effort: M (one D1 migration + ingest insert + runtime fusion fn). Measure: Hit-Rate@5 / Precision@5 on exact-term queries, dense vs hybrid.
3. **Eval `@cf/google/gemma-4-26b-a4b-it` as a generation drop-in (the cost lever). [P1]** 7.5x cheaper output ($0.30/M vs $2.25/M), 256K context. Gated on item 1. Run the gold set across ≥3 rulebooks incl. one with expansions; grade [N] citation formatting, expansion scoping, and the "not in my rulebook" fallback. Cut over only if it matches current quality. Impact: High ($ at scale on a public no-login app). Effort: L–M (model-id swap + eval run). Measure: gold-set quality parity + cost-per-answered-query drop.
4. **Make `--contextual` the default and re-index existing rulebooks — gated on item 1. [P1]** Proven large retrieval-failure reduction; ingest-only cost; ingest is idempotent. Validate before/after Hit-Rate@5 on at least one game *before* the global default, since the re-index re-embeds/re-upserts every vector (irreversible-ish). Pre-check the blurb-prefix token-budget interaction (prefix + ~2.5x local-tokenizer undercount can push chunks past the 1024 cap). Impact: High, esp. for games with expansions. Effort: M (remove flag gate; the blurb generator is a sequential per-chunk Moonshot loop; run ingest; watch Moonshot cost). Measure: Hit-Rate@5 before vs after on the gold set.
5. **Add faithfulness + citation-precision checks (RAGAS, Workers AI judge). [P2]** Retrieval metrics can't catch a Goblin that cites the right chunk but invents a detail, or attaches [N] to an unsupported sentence. Run RAGAS faithfulness on 30–50 generated rulings with llama-3.3-70b as judge (atomic-claim decomposition mitigates verbosity bias); use a free noun-phrase-overlap heuristic as a first-pass citation-precision filter. Impact: High (measures the core trust contract). Effort: M (~50-line RAGAS Workers AI adapter). Measure: mean faithfulness ≥0.90, citation precision ≥0.85.
6. **Add a context-window guard in `onChatMessage`. [P2]** The 24,000-token Workers AI cap (verified) can truncate very long sessions, but it is already bounded by `maxPersistedMessages = 100` + the 4h DO wipe + the 5-passage/600-output budget, so this is cheap reliability insurance, not a cost or guardrail risk. Prune oldest turns when estimated tokens approach ~20K. Note the local tokenizer diverges ~2.5x from the server count (ingest.ts:49-53), so use a tokenizer or a conservative margin, not naive char/4. Impact: Low–Medium (eliminates an edge case). Effort: L (~50 lines + token estimate). Measure: max-session-length probe; no truncated generations in logs.
7. **Terse-follow-up: experiment with always-fold-prior-turn for short queries (keep retry-on-empty as fallback). [P2]** This is a precision/latency *experiment*, not a clear win. Today retry-on-empty (agent.ts:125) only fires when pass 1 is empty, so on-topic direct questions already cost one embed+query; folding the prior turn unconditionally removes the retry *only when the fold succeeds where the bare query failed*, and risks polluting the embedding for short-but-NEW topic-switch questions. Gate it on the gold set's terse-subset Hit-Rate@5 AND a topic-switch decoy case; keep retry-on-empty as the safe fallback. Impact: Medium (if it holds up). Effort: L. Measure: terse-subset Hit-Rate@5 + topic-switch decoy precision; retry trigger rate.
8. **Production health logging (Workers Logs). [P3]** Log `{session_id, game_id, second_pass_used, answer_empty, retrieved_chunk_ids_count, tokens_used}` per query. Query monthly for empty-retrieval rate per game (a spike = re-check that game's index), second-pass rate, and refused-vs-answered ratio. Impact: Medium (leading indicators between offline evals). Effort: L. Measure: the dashboards themselves.

Explicitly **defer**: HyDE, step-back, query decomposition (each adds a billed pre-retrieval LLM call and re-introduces a generation call on the refusal path for negligible gain on a small, single-hop corpus); external rerankers; agentic redesign; embedding migration (until a measured trigger).

## 6. How to measure

Adopt a single offline harness that lives next to the ingest script and runs against the real embedding + Vectorize + D1 stack (no Worker round-trip needed):

- **Gold set per game** (item 1) — the foundation. Anchor every pair to `expected_chunk_ids` so retrieval scores independently of generation. Include unanswerable pairs to test the "That is not in my rulebook" guard, base-with-decoy-expansion pairs to regression-test scoping, and terse two-turn pairs to regression-test the follow-up handling.
- **Retrieval metrics (no generation call):** Hit-Rate@5 and Precision@5 (post-rerank), Recall@20 (ANN alone), and a separate *filter-hit-rate* (did expected_chunk_ids even survive the game_id filter — distinct from a retrieval miss). Run the reranker on vs off (cosine-ordered top-5 as baseline) to confirm it earns its latency on *your* corpus, and verify neither the 0.15 cosine floor nor the 0.2 rerank gate drops known-good chunks.
- **Scoping regression tests (deterministic):** Test A (expansion bleed) — for base-game questions where the answer also appears in an expansion, fraction where the top-cited chunk has `kind='base'`. Test B (expansion opt-in) — for explicit-expansion questions, fraction where an expansion chunk is in top-5.
- **Terse-follow-up tests:** with-fold vs without-fold Hit-Rate@5 on the terse subset, plus a topic-switch decoy case (item 7) to confirm folding doesn't hurt new short questions.
- **Answer-quality (LLM judge, sampled):** RAGAS faithfulness (target ≥0.90) and citation precision (target ≥0.85) on 30–50 rulings, judged via Workers AI llama-3.3-70b to stay on-account; decompose into atomic claims to blunt verbosity bias.
- **Production telemetry:** Workers Logs structured events, reviewed monthly (item 8).

Tooling: RAGAS for the answer-quality batch (one ~50-line Workers-AI-REST judge adapter); plain chunk-ID comparison for everything retrieval-side — no framework needed and no per-run inference cost. This is a few hours to stand up and turns every threshold and every prior fix into a guarded, repeatable number.

## 7. Critique resolution

- **Section 3 "NOT the in-catalog kimi-k2.7-code" (major) — FIXED.** Verified live: `@cf/moonshotai/kimi-k2.7-code` IS in the Workers AI catalog ($0.95/M in, $4.00/M out, 262K context). The report now states the ingest script calls Moonshot's external API directly even though an in-catalog equivalent exists, and that a fully first-party path is available.
- **"Documented tool-call hallucination on Workers AI" for llama-4-scout (major) — FIXED.** No primary source supports this, and Cloudflare's own chat-agent example uses Scout with tools. Removed the fabricated claim; the report now steers away from Scout only on the verifiable ground that it is a 17B MoE vs the 70B dense model (weaker strict-citation instruction-following).
- **kimi-k2.6 "3-4x"/"4x" output cost (minor) — FIXED.** Corrected to ~1.8x ($4.00/M vs $2.25/M).
- **qwen3 "drop-in" undersells re-embed cost (minor) — FIXED.** Reworded to "drop-in on the immutable dims/metric (1024/cosine), so no index re-creation — but still a full re-embed and re-upsert of every vector," in both the prose and the embedding verdict.
- **24K context cap nuance (minor) — APPLIED.** Verified 24,000 tokens on the model page; clarified that 128K is upstream and not selectable on Workers AI, so the guard is the only mitigation, and that it is already bounded by maxPersistedMessages + 4h wipe (demoting the guard to P2).
- **Refusal path "free / no model call" (major) — FIXED.** Restated precisely: the refusal path skips the generation call and the daily-budget slot but still pays embed + Vectorize (+ possibly rerank) once it clears the rate limiters; the daily budget counts only in-scope queries; abuse is bounded by the rate limiters, not zero cost. This strengthens the anti-agentic argument.
- **Item 4 terse-fold "removes the second retrieval pass" overstated (minor) — APPLIED.** Reframed as a precision/latency experiment gated on the terse subset plus a topic-switch decoy, with retry-on-empty kept as fallback; demoted to P2.
- **Item 2 contextual-default effort understated (minor) — APPLIED.** Re-labelled Effort Medium, gated on item 1, with the sequential-Moonshot-loop and blurb-prefix-token-budget pre-checks called out; sequenced as P1-after-P0, not co-equal P0.
- **Ferrazzi paper venue/figures unverified (minor) — APPLIED.** Attributed by title + arXiv id, presented the multipliers as reported-not-settled, and noted the conclusion does not hinge on them.
- **Backlog ordering (minor) — APPLIED.** Re-sequenced: P0 eval harness → P1 FTS5 hybrid → P1 Gemma 4 eval → P1 contextual default → P2 faithfulness/context-guard/terse-fold → P3 logging.
- **Speculative reranker ids, return_sparse/D1 FTS5 grounding, "not deprecated" (minor, no change needed) — confirmed.** The speculative reranker ids are kept explicitly conditional; the return_sparse-unavailable + D1-FTS5-on-Cloudflare claims verified; the deprecation status strengthened with the May 2026 changelog citation.
